subject: Chemistry
title: Cochineal Red: Color From Bugs
description: A micro-lesson that makes Cochineal Red: Color From Bugs usable.

hook.text: The bright red in your Starbucks drink may come from crushed insects. Cochineal dye is made from scale bugs.

buildup.text: Cochineal insects live on cactus in Central America. They produce carminic acid to repel predators.

discovery.text: Crushing 70,000 dried insects yields one pound of dye. The Aztecs valued it almost as highly as gold.

twist.text: After Spain conquered the Aztecs, cochineal became the most valuable export after silver. A color funded an empire.

climax.text: Cochineal is still used in food (E120), cosmetics, and fabric. It's one of the safest red dyes known.

punchline.text: Crushed bugs make brilliant red. Still in your food, lipstick, and drinks.

quiz.question: What produces the red color in cochineal dye?
options: Carminic acid from crushed scale insects on cactus plants
Iron oxide extracted from desert sand
A synthetic pigment invented by Spanish chemists
